review.method: Reuses the checked-in manual question-and-mark-evidence decisions in scripts/lib/aqa-science-topic-mapping.mjs; no prompt classifier or model call.

sourceDocument.subject: Biology
sourceDocument.title: AQA GCSE Biology 8461/1H Question Paper November 2020

question.selfContainedPromptText:
Students investigated the effect of temperature on the rate of photosynthesis. Table 1 shows the volume of oxygen produced per hour at different temperatures. X is the mean for 20 °C.

Calculate mean value X.
question.contextText: Students investigated the effect of temperature on the rate of photosynthesis. Table 1 shows the volume of oxygen produced per hour at different temperatures. X is the mean for 20 °C.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Mean
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: A mean is calculated as the total of the relevant repeat readings divided by the number of readings used.

question.promptText: Complete Figure 1 using data from Table 1.
question.selfContainedPromptText:
Table 1 gives the mean rate of photosynthesis at temperatures from 20 °C to 45 °C. Figure 1 is a blank graph with temperature in °C on the x-axis.

Complete Figure 1 using data from Table 1.
question.contextText: Table 1 gives the mean rate of photosynthesis at temperatures from 20 °C to 45 °C. Figure 1 is a blank graph with temperature in °C on the x-axis.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Graph Plotting
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: axis label -> scale -> plots -> best-fit curve
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Complete the graph by labelling the y-axis with the measured quantity and unit, using a suitable scale, plotting the table values accurately, and adding the correct best-fit line.

markSchemeItems.text: All mean points plotted to within ± half a small square earns 2 marks; allow 3 or 4 correct plots for 1 mark. Ignore any attempt to plot a point at 20 °C.

markSchemeItems.text: Correct curved line of best fit; ignore straight lines joining point to point and ignore extrapolation.

question.promptText: Suggest two other factors which the scientists would have controlled.
question.selfContainedPromptText:
Data from The Million Women survey in the UK was collected for over 15 years. Scientists analysed the data to study the effect of consuming alcohol on liver disease. The scientists included 400 000 women who regularly consumed alcohol, included 400 000 women who did not consume alcohol, and excluded women who already had a liver disease. Age and gender were two factors controlled in this analysis, and many other factors were also controlled.

Suggest two other factors which the scientists would have controlled.
question.contextText: Data from The Million Women survey in the UK was collected for over 15 years. Scientists analysed the data to study the effect of consuming alcohol on liver disease. The scientists included 400 000 women who regularly consumed alcohol, included 400 000 women who did not consume alcohol, and excluded women who already had a liver disease. Age and gender were two factors controlled in this analysis, and many other factors were also controlled.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Extra controls
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: named controls -> other confounders -> two distinct

sourceDocument.subject: Biology
sourceDocument.title: AQA GCSE Biology 8461/1H Question Paper November 2020

question.promptText: Calculate the percentage decrease in relative risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver for this woman.
question.selfContainedPromptText:
Data from The Million Women survey in the UK was collected for over 15 years. Scientists analysed the data to study the effect of consuming alcohol on liver disease. The scientists included 400 000 women who regularly consumed alcohol, included 400 000 women who did not consume alcohol, and excluded women who already had a liver disease. Age and gender were two factors controlled in this analysis, and many other factors were also controlled. The data was analysed for women who drank alcohol with meals, women who drank alcohol not with meals, and women who did not drink alcohol. During the survey approximately 1500 women developed a liver disease called cirrhosis of the liver. Scientists calculated the relative risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver for each group who consumed alcohol. A relative risk of 1.0 means there was no statistical difference between the groups who did consume alcohol and the group who did not consume alcohol. Figure 10 shows a summary of the results. A woman drinks 150 g of alcohol per week not with meals. The woman decides to change to drinking 150 g of alcohol per week with meals.

Calculate the percentage decrease in relative risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver for this woman.
question.contextText: Data from The Million Women survey in the UK was collected for over 15 years. Scientists analysed the data to study the effect of consuming alcohol on liver disease. The scientists included 400 000 women who regularly consumed alcohol, included 400 000 women who did not consume alcohol, and excluded women who already had a liver disease. Age and gender were two factors controlled in this analysis, and many other factors were also controlled. The data was analysed for women who drank alcohol with meals, women who drank alcohol not with meals, and women who did not drink alcohol. During the survey approximately 1500 women developed a liver disease called cirrhosis of the liver. Scientists calculated the relative risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver for each group who consumed alcohol. A relative risk of 1.0 means there was no statistical difference between the groups who did consume alcohol and the group who did not consume alcohol. Figure 10 shows a summary of the results. A woman drinks 150 g of alcohol per week not with meals. The woman decides to change to drinking 150 g of alcohol per week with meals.

sourceDocument.title: AQA GCSE Biology 8461/1H Mark Scheme November 2020

markSchemeItems.text: 2.55 - 1.60 = 0.95; allow 1.60 - 2.55 = -0.95 and graph readings in accepted ranges.

sourceDocument.title: AQA GCSE Biology 8461/1H Mark Scheme November 2020

markSchemeItems.text: 0.95 / 2.55 × 100 = 37(.2549019608...)%; allow answer correctly calculated from accepted graph readings, including -37%.

question.promptText: Calculate the relative risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver for this woman.
question.selfContainedPromptText:
Data from The Million Women survey in the UK was collected for over 15 years. Scientists analysed the data to study the effect of consuming alcohol on liver disease. The scientists included 400 000 women who regularly consumed alcohol, included 400 000 women who did not consume alcohol, and excluded women who already had a liver disease. Age and gender were two factors controlled in this analysis, and many other factors were also controlled. The data was analysed for women who drank alcohol with meals, women who drank alcohol not with meals, and women who did not drink alcohol. During the survey approximately 1500 women developed a liver disease called cirrhosis of the liver. Scientists calculated the relative risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver for each group who consumed alcohol. A relative risk of 1.0 means there was no statistical difference between the groups who did consume alcohol and the group who did not consume alcohol. Figure 10 shows a summary of the results. One glass of wine contains 12 g of alcohol. A different woman drinks two glasses of wine each day with her meals.

Calculate the relative risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver for this woman.
question.contextText: Data from The Million Women survey in the UK was collected for over 15 years. Scientists analysed the data to study the effect of consuming alcohol on liver disease. The scientists included 400 000 women who regularly consumed alcohol, included 400 000 women who did not consume alcohol, and excluded women who already had a liver disease. Age and gender were two factors controlled in this analysis, and many other factors were also controlled. The data was analysed for women who drank alcohol with meals, women who drank alcohol not with meals, and women who did not drink alcohol. During the survey approximately 1500 women developed a liver disease called cirrhosis of the liver. Scientists calculated the relative risk of developing cirrhosis of the liver for each group who consumed alcohol. A relative risk of 1.0 means there was no statistical difference between the groups who did consume alcohol and the group who did not consume alcohol. Figure 10 shows a summary of the results. One glass of wine contains 12 g of alcohol. A different woman drinks two glasses of wine each day with her meals.
